# B21: Key Quotes & Strategic Questions

## Key Quotes

**"What jobs are is learning — is getting paid to learn. But unfortunately, there's not that many jobs out there."**
— Maximilian Kwok
*Context:* Articulating the core thesis behind his product — that the traditional gateway to professional experience (employment) has narrowed, creating a need for simulation-based alternatives. This is the philosophical anchor of his entire venture.

**"On our marketplace, it's a marketplace of all possible futures that you can dream up for yourself."**
— Maximilian Kwok
*Context:* Describing the product's positioning. Reframes career simulation from a training tool into an aspirational experience — "futures" rather than "assessments" or "courses." Reveals deliberate messaging strategy to appeal to the emotional side of career change.

**"If you sell to everyone, you sell to no one."**
— Maximilian Kwok
*Context:* Acknowledging the discipline required to resist chasing the mass market. Said in response to V's observation about the temptation of the large resume-generation market versus the smaller but more defensible market for novel hiring signals.

**"The difference between knowing and understanding can be captured by so many ideas in startup. Product market fit was another one — I kept hearing it and I understood intellectually what it meant. But when you really know how to feel it — very different thing."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Reflecting on the experiential nature of founder knowledge. This crystallizes a recurring theme in V's thinking — that startup wisdom is embodied, not conceptual. Applies broadly to mentoring and coaching work.

**"Nobody's — it's in no one's interest to create the infra for transmitting the signal because there's no real economic reward for acquiring and processing the signal to the extent and to the standard that I think you and I would agree needs to be had to properly evaluate human beings at scale."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Diagnosing why the hiring ecosystem remains broken — a tragedy of the commons where building proper evaluation infrastructure has no clear economic incentive. Frames the structural problem both companies are trying to solve.

**"Workday's telling us that they have dial-up and they're saying this is good enough — when we know broadband is what's needed."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Vivid analogy comparing current HR tech infrastructure to dial-up internet. Positions both Careerspan and Max's product as broadband-era solutions fighting against incumbent complacency.

**"The same person could be a once-in-a-lifetime hire for one role and a generational mistake of a hire for another role. It could be the same person."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Arguing that evaluation is always contextual — the axes you choose to measure on are a function of the role, not universal. Undercuts the premise of standardized assessments and reinforces the need for role-specific signal.

**"Whatever you do in your work has to have some sort of visible link between you and the service or product that's being sold. Because otherwise — what differentiates you from an AI?"**
— Maximilian Kwok
*Context:* Articulating the existential question facing knowledge workers in 2026. If your work is abstracted away from the product, AI can replace you. This is the motivating insight behind his product's design — helping people demonstrate direct product impact.

## Strategic Questions Raised

**"What do you think is the biggest obstacle to revenue generation right now?"**
— Maximilian Kwok
*Significance:* Direct, strategic question that surfaced V's core challenge — marketplace bootstrapping and the two-sided problem. Showed genuine engagement and led to V's candid discussion of Careerspan's current state and pivot strategy.

**"Are you envisioning this to be an assessment in the sense that you are evaluating them, or is it meant to be a means for them to create this portfolio that is then the thing that they are evaluated on?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* Forced a critical product distinction. Max's answer — portfolio first, assessment second — revealed strategic wisdom about avoiding the assessment trap. V's follow-up ("assessments are a fucking hard thing to pull off effectively") validated the choice.

**"Are you expecting companies to pay to be surfaced high-quality candidates?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* Probed the monetization model directly. Max's response that this is a future revenue stream, not the initial one, revealed the product's usage-based monetization strategy (energy credits for AI deployment) — a more scalable approach than B2B enterprise sales.
